name: language-choice
title: Choose the primary implementation language
description: Pick the main programming language for the project. Drives hiring profile, library ecosystem, runtime characteristics, and tooling.

starter.summary: Decide the primary implementation language for the project.
starter.issue: Every other foundational decision (runtime, package manager, framework choices, testing tools) flows from the language choice. Picking this early and explicitly avoids drift.
assumptions: Team has existing language strengths to lean on.
Project lifespan is long enough that hiring and onboarding matter.
Ecosystem maturity matters for the project's domain.
constraints: Target runtime environments (browser, server, native, embedded).
Licensing or compliance restrictions on language ecosystems.

positions.title: TypeScript
positions.description: Strongly typed JavaScript. Best for full-stack web work, ubiquitous tooling.
cons: Slower than native languages for hot paths

positions.title: Python
positions.description: Dynamic, batteries-included. Best for data work, scripting, ML, fast prototypes.

positions.title: Go
positions.description: Statically typed, compiled, built for concurrent services.
cons: Less rich third-party ecosystem than JS/Python

positions.title: Rust
positions.description: Memory-safe systems language. Best for performance-critical or systems work.

implications: Hiring profile shifts to match the chosen language.
Library ecosystem and framework choices are bounded by the language.
Runtime target follows (e.g., Node for TS, Python interpreter, Go binary, Rust binary).
CI/CD and tooling must support the chosen language.

notes_for_agent: Before instantiating, ask: what does the team know best? What's the workload (web app vs data pipeline vs systems tool)? What's the deployment target? Customize 'positions' to drop options that don't apply and add language-specific framework expectations. Stretch the assumptions section to be project-specific.
